walkthrough.title: Choose Your Game Mode
walkthrough.text: Before anything else, your group needs to decide between Standard Mode and Expert Mode. Standard Mode uses 12 sectors and is recommended for your first game. Expert Mode uses 18 sectors and is significantly more challenging. If everyone at the table is new to the game, go with Standard Mode. If your group has experience with deduction games or logic puzzles, feel free to jump into Expert Mode.

walkthrough.title: Place the Solar System Board
walkthrough.text: Place the solar system board in the center of the table. Make sure you are using the correct side of the board for your chosen game mode — one side is Standard, the other is Expert. Orient the board so that each player is viewing it from one of the four sides marked with a unique icon: spring equinox, summer solstice, autumn equinox, or winter solstice. Each player needs to see the board from their own icon's perspective.

walkthrough.title: Assemble the Sun Disc and Earth Board
walkthrough.text: Place the sun disc so that it sits in the hole in the center of the solar system board. Then place the earth board on top of the solar system board with the sun disc fitting in its center hole. Rotate the earth board so that the visible sky starts with sector 1. The earth board has a cutaway section that reveals sector numbers on the inner ring — this is the visible sky. It always shows exactly half the total sectors: 6 sectors in Standard Mode, 9 in Expert Mode.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Player Pawns on the Time Track
walkthrough.text: Each player chooses a color. Find the player pawns matching the chosen colors. Place all of these pawns in sector 1 of the time track, which runs around the outer edge of the solar system board. Line the pawns up in random order. The time track is how the game determines turn order — the player whose pawn is farthest back is always the active player.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Note Sheets and Pencils
walkthrough.text: Give each player a pencil and a note sheet. Each note sheet has a unique icon — spring equinox, summer solstice, autumn equinox, or winter solstice — and each player must use the note sheet matching their view of the board. Also make sure all players are using the side of the note sheet that corresponds to the chosen game mode. The note sheet is where you will record all clues, deductions, and logic throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Player Screens
walkthrough.text: Give each player a player screen matching their chosen player color. Each player positions their screen so that it hides their note sheet from other players. Your deductions are private — no one else should see what you have figured out. In Expert Mode, also give each player the expert panel matching their screen and slide it into the slots in the player screen.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Tokens
walkthrough.text: Give each player two target tokens and the 12 theory tokens matching their chosen player color. Each player puts all their tokens behind their player screen. In Standard Mode, each player uses only one of their dwarf planet theory tokens — return the other three dwarf planet tokens to the game box. In Expert Mode, each player uses all four dwarf planet theory tokens.

walkthrough.title: Install and Launch the Companion App
walkthrough.text: Each player needs access to the companion app on an electronic device. Players can share devices if needed. Install and launch the app on all devices by visiting www.PlanetXApp.com. On one device, press Start New Game, select the game mode, and press Start Game to generate a game code. On each other device, press Enter Game Code and type in the code, then press Join Game. Verify all devices show the same game code, then press Continue.

walkthrough.title: Receive Starting Information
walkthrough.text: Each player will now receive starting information from the app based on their chosen experience level. If you have not played before, choose Beginner for more starting clues. Experienced players can try the Experienced or Genius level for a tougher challenge. Follow the instructions in the app to receive your starting information and record it on your note sheet. This information gives you an initial foothold for your deductions.

walkthrough.title: Start the Game
walkthrough.text: Once all players have recorded their starting information, press the Start Game button on all devices. You are now ready to begin! Remember: the player whose pawn is farthest back on the time track always goes first. Since everyone starts in sector 1, the pawn in the rearmost position of the stack goes first.

walkthrough.title: How Turn Order Works
walkthrough.text: Unlike most board games, The Search for Planet X does not use a fixed turn order. Instead, the player whose pawn is farthest back on the time track is always the active player. When it is your turn, you take one action, advance your pawn clockwise on the time track by the action's time cost, then check if the earth board needs to rotate. Actions with higher time costs push your pawn farther ahead, meaning other players may get multiple turns before you go again. This creates an interesting tension — powerful actions cost more time.

walkthrough.title: The Four Actions Overview
walkthrough.text: On your turn, you choose one of four actions. Survey for an Object lets you scan a range of visible sectors for a specific object type. Target a Sector reveals exactly what is in one visible sector. Research a Topic gives you a logic rule about how objects relate to each other. And Locate Planet X is your attempt to win the game by identifying Planet X's sector and its two neighbors. Each action requires interacting with the companion app, and the information you receive is private — record it on your note sheet but do not share it with other players.

walkthrough.title: Action: Survey for an Object
walkthrough.text: Press the Survey button in the app. Select a type of object — asteroid, comet, gas cloud, dwarf planet, or truly empty — then select a range of consecutive sectors in the visible sky. Announce your object type and sector range to the other players. The app reveals how many objects of that type are in that range. This number is private. Important: the sector containing Planet X appears empty in surveys, so if you survey for empty sectors, Planet X's sector may be included in the count. For comets, the range must start and end with a sector that can contain a comet.

walkthrough.title: Survey Time Costs
walkthrough.text: The time cost of a survey depends on the range size. Surveys of 1, 2, or 3 sectors cost 4 time. Surveys of 4, 5, or 6 sectors cost 3 time. In Expert Mode only, surveys of 7, 8, or 9 sectors cost 2 time. The visible sky in Standard Mode is only 6 sectors wide, so the maximum survey range is 6 sectors. A survey can start and end in the same sector to cover just 1 sector, but consider using the Target action instead for single sectors.

walkthrough.title: Action: Target a Sector
walkthrough.text: Press the Target button in the app. Select one sector in the visible sky and announce your selection to the other players. The app reveals which object is located in that sector, or that the sector appears empty. This information is private. You must spend one of your two target tokens to take this action. Once both tokens are spent, you cannot target again for the rest of the game. The time cost is 4. Remember: the sector containing Planet X appears empty when targeted — an empty result could mean truly empty or Planet X.

walkthrough.title: Action: Research a Topic
walkthrough.text: Press the Research button in the app and select one of the available research topics, which are based on object types. Announce your selected topic to the other players. The app reveals a logic rule that applies to this particular game — for example, a relationship between certain objects and sectors. This is private information; record it on your note sheet. The time cost is only 1, making this the cheapest action. However, you cannot research twice in a row. If you research on one turn, you must choose a different action next turn. Each topic reveals only one piece of information, so there is no reason to research the same topic again.

walkthrough.title: Action: Locate Planet X
walkthrough.text: This is the big one. Press the Locate Planet X button in the app. Select which sector you think contains Planet X, then select which objects you believe are in the two adjacent sectors. The app reveals whether you are correct or incorrect. Announce whether you succeeded or failed to the other players, but do not announce which sector you guessed. The time cost is 5, the most expensive action. If you are correct, you have triggered the end of the game. After advancing your pawn, skip directly to end-of-game procedures — do not rotate the earth board.

walkthrough.title: Advancing Your Pawn
walkthrough.text: After taking your action, move your pawn clockwise on the time track by a number of sectors equal to the time cost. Locate Planet X costs 5, Target a Sector costs 4, Survey of 1-3 sectors costs 4, Survey of 4-6 sectors costs 3, Survey of 7-9 sectors costs 2 (Expert only), and Research a Topic costs 1. If your pawn lands in a sector already containing other pawns, place yours ahead of them but still in that same sector — those other players will take their turns before you.

walkthrough.title: Rotating the Earth Board
walkthrough.text: After advancing your pawn, check if the earth board needs to rotate. If the arrow on the earth board is not pointing at the sector containing the pawn farthest back on the time track, rotate the earth board clockwise, one sector at a time. As you rotate, watch for conference icons and theory icons. When the arrow reaches a conference icon, pause to perform a conference phase. When it reaches a theory icon, pause for a theory phase. Continue rotating until the arrow reaches the sector of the farthest-back pawn.

walkthrough.title: Conference Phase
walkthrough.text: When the earth board reaches a conference icon during rotation, all players receive a new piece of information about Planet X's location. Press the Planet X Conference button in the app and select the matching conference icon — either X1 or X2. The app reveals a logic rule for this game. Unlike other information, this is announced to all players. Everyone records it on their note sheet. Each conference is only performed once — if the earth board passes the same icon on a later rotation, skip it.

walkthrough.title: Theory Phase: Submitting Theories
walkthrough.text: When the earth board reaches a theory icon during rotation, players may submit theories. A theory is a claim about which object is in a specific sector. In Standard Mode, each player may submit up to 1 theory per theory phase; in Expert Mode, up to 2. All players simultaneously select their theories behind their screens, then place their hand over them. In player order (back to front on the time track), each player places their selected theory tokens face down on the outer space of the peer review track in the chosen sector. You can submit theories in any sector, not just the visible sky, but not in a sector already confirmed by peer review.

walkthrough.title: Theory Phase: Advancing and Peer Review
walkthrough.text: After all theories are placed, advance all face-down theories — both new and old — one space inward on their peer review tracks. Each sector's peer review track has four spaces. When a theory reaches the inner space, it undergoes peer review. Press the Peer Review button in the app, select the sector and the object shown on the theory. The app reveals if the theory is correct or incorrect — announce this to all players.

walkthrough.title: Resolving Peer Reviews
walkthrough.text: If a theory is correct, the object in that sector is now confirmed. Every player circles that object on their note sheet. No more theories can be placed in that sector. All other theories in that sector are flipped face up — correct ones remain for scoring, and incorrect ones are removed. Players with incorrect theories advance their pawn 1 sector as a penalty. If a theory is incorrect, remove it from the game and the player advances 1 sector as a penalty. The sector remains unconfirmed and other face-down theories stay hidden.

walkthrough.title: End of Game Trigger
walkthrough.text: The game ends when one player correctly locates Planet X using the Locate Planet X action. That player advances their pawn by the time cost of 5 but does not rotate the earth board. The player who found Planet X should not announce its location. Each other player whose pawn is at least 1 sector behind the finder gets one final scoring opportunity, taken in player order (back to front).

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring Opportunities
walkthrough.text: Based on how far back your pawn is from the player who located Planet X, you may either submit theories or attempt to locate Planet X yourself. If you are 1, 2, or 3 sectors back, you may submit 1 theory. If you are 4 or 5 sectors back, you may submit up to 2 theories. Alternatively, you may attempt to Locate Planet X — if correct, you score 2 to 10 points based on how far back your pawn is. Players do not advance their pawns during these final opportunities.

walkthrough.title: Reveal Objects and Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: After all final opportunities, press Reveal Objects in the app. The contents of every sector are displayed. Announce them to all players. Flip all remaining face-down theories face up and check them. Now tally final scores. Score 1 leader bonus point for each sector where you were the first player (or tied for first) to submit the correct theory. Score points for each correct theory: asteroids 2 points, comets 3 points, gas clouds 4 points, dwarf planets 4 points in Standard or 2 points each in Expert. Score 10 points for locating Planet X first, or 2 points per sector back if you located it later. The highest score wins.

walkthrough.title: Tiebreakers
walkthrough.text: If players are tied on total score, the tied player who scored the most points for locating Planet X wins. If still tied, the player who scored the most leader bonus points wins. If still tied, the tied players share the victory.

Higher time cost = fewer turns — powerful actions have a price

walkthrough.title: Welcome and Meet the Players
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted opening with three example players — the recommended player count for The Search for Planet X. Player 1 is Blue, Player 2 is Yellow, and Player 3 is Red. We will use Standard Mode with 12 sectors. Each player has their note sheet, player screen, 2 target tokens, and their theory tokens behind their screen. The app has been set up and each player has received their starting clues. All three pawns are in sector 1 on the time track. Let's begin.

walkthrough.title: Understanding the Board
walkthrough.text: Take a moment to look at the board. The solar system has 12 sectors arranged in a circle. The earth board sits on top, and its cutaway shows the visible sky — sectors 1 through 6 right now. The time track runs around the outer edge. You will also see theory icons and conference icons marked along the track at certain sectors. As the earth board rotates throughout the game, the visible sky shifts and these phase triggers activate.

walkthrough.title: What We Know So Far
walkthrough.text: Before we start taking actions, let's review what the rulebook tells us about the solar system. There are 12 objects placed across the 12 sectors: 4 asteroids, 2 comets, 2 gas clouds, 1 dwarf planet, 1 Planet X, and 2 truly empty sectors. Each object type follows specific logic rules. Comets can only be in sectors 2, 3, 5, 7, or 11 in Standard Mode. Asteroids must be adjacent to at least one other asteroid. Gas clouds must be adjacent to at least one truly empty sector. No dwarf planet is adjacent to Planet X. And Planet X appears empty in surveys and targets.

walkthrough.title: Blue's First Turn: Research a Topic
walkthrough.text: Blue is farthest back on the time track, so Blue goes first. Blue decides to start with a Research action — it only costs 1 time, so it keeps Blue near the back of the track for more frequent turns. Blue presses the Research button in the app and selects a topic about asteroids. The app reveals a logic rule, something like 'All asteroids are in consecutive sectors.' Blue records this on their note sheet. This is huge — it means all 4 asteroids are in one unbroken group rather than two pairs. Blue announces they researched asteroids, but keeps the result secret.

walkthrough.title: Why Research First Is Smart
walkthrough.text: Research costs only 1 time, which means Blue barely moves on the time track. Blue will likely get to take another turn soon, possibly even before the other players. Research clues also apply to the entire board, not just the visible sky, making them powerful for narrowing down possibilities across all 12 sectors. Starting with research is one of the strongest opening moves in the game.

walkthrough.title: Yellow's First Turn: Survey for Asteroids
walkthrough.text: Yellow is next. Yellow decides to survey for asteroids in sectors 1 through 4 — a 4-sector range in the visible sky. Yellow announces the survey type and range, then checks the app. The app says there are 2 asteroids in that range. Yellow records this privately. A 4-sector survey costs 3 time, so Yellow advances their pawn 3 sectors on the time track.

walkthrough.title: Red's First Turn: The Mistake — Targeting Too Early
walkthrough.text: Red is eager to get concrete answers. Red decides to Target sector 3 to find out exactly what is there. Red spends one of their two target tokens, presses the Target button, and selects sector 3. The app reveals that sector 3 contains a comet. Red records this. The time cost is 4, so Red advances their pawn 4 sectors. But here is the problem — Red just used one of only two target tokens on the very first turn, and spent 4 time doing it. Worse, Red could have gotten similar information from a cheaper survey.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Conserve Your Target Tokens
walkthrough.text: You only get two target tokens for the entire game. Once they are gone, you can never target again. Targeting costs 4 time, the same as a small survey, but a survey covers multiple sectors and gives you broader information. Save your targets for late in the game when you need to confirm a single critical sector — like verifying what is adjacent to where you think Planet X is, right before you attempt to locate it. Using a target in the first few turns is almost always a mistake.

walkthrough.title: Blue's Second Turn: Another Research
walkthrough.text: Because Research only cost 1 time, Blue is already the farthest-back player again. Blue takes another turn — but wait, Blue cannot research two turns in a row. That is a restriction in the rules. Blue decides to survey for empty sectors in sectors 1 through 3 instead. The app says there is 1 empty sector in that range. Blue records this and advances 4 sectors. Remember, Planet X appears empty in surveys, so that 1 could be a truly empty sector or it could be Planet X.

walkthrough.title: Yellow's Second Turn: Research
walkthrough.text: Yellow takes a Research action, choosing a topic about gas clouds. The app reveals something like 'No gas cloud is directly opposite Planet X.' Yellow notes this on their note sheet and advances only 1 sector. Yellow is now well-positioned on the time track and has two solid clues to start making deductions.

walkthrough.title: Red's Mistake: Surveying Too Narrow
walkthrough.text: Red surveys for comets in sectors 5 through 5 — a single-sector survey. Red already knows sector 3 has a comet (from the target), so there are 2 comets total and Red wants to find the other one. The app says there is 0 comets in sector 5. Red now knows sector 5 does not have a comet. But this cost 4 time for eliminating just one sector. A wider survey of sectors 5 through 7 would have also cost 4 time but covered three possible comet sectors at once.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Maximize Your Survey Range
walkthrough.text: Surveys of 1, 2, or 3 sectors all cost the same — 4 time. And surveys of 4, 5, or 6 sectors all cost 3 time. So there is never a reason to survey fewer sectors within a cost bracket. Always survey the maximum number of sectors for the time you are spending. A 3-sector survey gives you three times the information of a 1-sector survey for the exact same cost. Think about what range gives you the most useful deductions before you press the button.

walkthrough.title: The Earth Board Rotates — Theory Phase
walkthrough.text: After these turns, the earth board needs to rotate to catch up with the farthest-back pawn. As it rotates sector by sector, it crosses a theory icon. Time for a theory phase! In Standard Mode, each player can submit up to 1 theory. Blue is confident that sector 3 has a comet based on deductions, so Blue places a comet theory token face down on sector 3's outer peer review space. Yellow and Red also select theories. Theories are placed in player order — back to front on the time track.

walkthrough.title: Advancing Theories on the Peer Review Track
walkthrough.text: After all theories are placed, we advance all face-down theories one space inward on their peer review tracks. Blue's theory on sector 3 moves from the outer space to the next space inward. It will take a few more theory phases before it reaches the inner space for peer review. Submitting theories early means they get reviewed sooner and you earn leader bonus points for being first. This is a key part of scoring — do not wait too long to submit theories you feel confident about.

walkthrough.title: Red's Redemption: Making Deductions
walkthrough.text: Despite the early mistakes, Red has useful information. Red knows sector 3 is a comet. Red knows from the starting clues which sectors the other comet could be in. Now Red applies logic — comets can only be in sectors 2, 3, 5, 7, or 11. One is in sector 3, so the other must be in 2, 5, 7, or 11. Red's earlier survey eliminated sector 5. A few more surveys and research clues and Red will narrow it down. This is the heart of the game — combining multiple pieces of information through logical deduction to eliminate possibilities.

walkthrough.title: Using Your Note Sheet Effectively
walkthrough.text: Your note sheet has a grid with rows for each object type and columns for each sector. As you gather information, cross out impossible combinations and circle confirmed ones. If a survey reveals 0 asteroids in sectors 1-3, cross out the asteroid row for sectors 1, 2, and 3. If research tells you all asteroids are consecutive, you can narrow down which groups of 4 consecutive sectors could hold them. The more you cross out, the fewer possibilities remain, and eventually you can deduce exactly what is in each sector.

walkthrough.title: The Conference Phase
walkthrough.text: As the earth board continues rotating, it reaches a conference icon. Everyone pauses for the conference phase. Press Planet X Conference in the app and select X1. The app reveals something like 'Planet X is not in sector 5 or sector 11.' This is announced to everyone — it is the only shared public information in the game besides what players announce about their actions. Every player records this clue. Conferences are powerful because they help everyone, but a savvy player who has already narrowed Planet X down can benefit more from eliminating the last few options.

walkthrough.title: Preparing to Locate Planet X
walkthrough.text: As the game progresses, you will accumulate enough clues to start narrowing down Planet X's location. Remember, to successfully Locate Planet X you need to know three things: which sector Planet X is in, what is in the sector to its left, and what is in the sector to its right. This is why you should save your target tokens — use them near the end to confirm the two adjacent sectors right before you make your attempt. The Locate action costs 5 time, the most expensive in the game, so you do not want to guess wrong.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have seen all four actions in practice, watched Red learn from two common mistakes — wasting a target token early and surveying too narrow — and you understand how the theory and conference phases work. You know how to use your note sheet to track deductions, and you know what it takes to locate Planet X. The rest of the game is about gathering more clues, making tighter deductions, and submitting theories for points along the way. Ready to play a real game?

walkthrough.title: Before You Launch
walkthrough.text:
Before you start your game, here are three things that can help.

First — check out General Tips for strategy basics on when to research vs. survey and how to manage your time track position.

Second — reference the Appendix for a quick guide to all object types, logic rules, spatial terms, and scoring details.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun finding Planet X!

Only costs 1 time — Yellow stays well-positioned on time track

summary.title: Red's Mistake: Surveying Too Narrow
bullets: Red surveys comets in sector 5 only — a 1-sector range
Costs 4 time but only eliminates one sector
Same cost would have covered sectors 5-7 (three sectors)

summary.title: Lesson: Maximize Your Survey Range
bullets: 1-3 sectors all cost 4 time — always survey 3 at that price
4-6 sectors all cost 3 time — always survey 6 at that price
More sectors per survey = more deduction power per time spent

Theories placed face down in player order (back to front on time track)

summary.title: Advancing Theories on the Peer Review Track
bullets: All face-down theories advance 1 space inward after each theory phase
4 spaces on the track — takes multiple phases to reach peer review
Submit early to earn leader bonus points for being first

bullets: Combine multiple clues: known sectors + logic rules + survey results
Eliminate impossibilities to narrow down what is in each sector
This is the heart of the game — logical deduction from partial information

bullets: Grid with object types (rows) x sectors (columns)
Cross out impossible combinations, circle confirmed ones
Combine survey results + research rules + conference clues for deductions

summary.title: The Conference Phase
bullets: Earth board reaches conference icon — triggers conference phase
App reveals a Planet X clue announced to ALL players
Only public shared information in the game besides action announcements

summary.title: Preparing to Locate Planet X
bullets: Need to know Planet X's sector AND both adjacent sectors' contents
Save target tokens for confirming adjacent sectors before your attempt
Locate costs 5 time — the most expensive action, so guess right

summary.title: Congratulations
bullets: Practiced all 4 actions: Research, Survey, Target, Locate Planet X
Watched Red learn from mistakes: wasting a target early, surveying too narrow
Understood theory phases, conference phases, and note sheet deductions

summary.title: Before You Launch
bullets: Check out General Tips for strategy basics on time management and action choices
Reference the Appendix for object types, logic rules, and scoring details
Ask your personal GM Guide — tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we get into strategy, here are the most common mistakes new players make. First, using target tokens too early — you only have two for the entire game, and they are most valuable at the end when confirming sectors adjacent to Planet X. Second, surveying a narrow range when a wider one costs the same time. Third, ignoring theory submissions and missing out on leader bonus points and theory scoring. Fourth, rushing to Locate Planet X without confirming the adjacent sectors, resulting in a wrong guess that costs 5 time and reveals your failure to everyone. Keep these in mind and you will avoid the biggest beginner traps.

walkthrough.title: The Power of Research
walkthrough.text: Research is the cheapest action at only 1 time cost. It gives you logic rules that apply to the entire board, not just the visible sky. Early in the game, research is extremely powerful because each rule narrows down possibilities across all 12 or 18 sectors simultaneously. A good opening pattern is to research on your first turn, then take a different action, then research again. The restriction that you cannot research twice in a row means you should plan your turns in research-other-research pairs. By the mid-game, you should have researched most available topics.

walkthrough.title: Survey Efficiency
walkthrough.text: Always survey the maximum number of sectors within each cost bracket. Surveys of 1, 2, or 3 sectors all cost 4 time. Surveys of 4, 5, or 6 sectors all cost 3 time. In Expert Mode, surveys of 7, 8, or 9 sectors cost 2 time. So a 3-sector survey is three times more efficient than a 1-sector survey for the same price. When choosing your range, think about which result would give you the most useful deduction. A result of 0 is just as useful as any other number — it eliminates possibilities across the entire range.

walkthrough.title: Time Track Management
walkthrough.text: The time track is the hidden engine of the game. The farthest-back player always goes first, so taking cheap actions keeps you in control of your turn frequency. A player who takes nothing but 1-cost research actions will get many more turns than a player who surveys for 4 time each turn. But more turns do not automatically mean more information — balance cheap actions with more expensive ones that give you critical data. The ideal is to stay near the back of the time track in the early game while still gathering useful information.

walkthrough.title: When to Submit Theories
walkthrough.text: Theories are a huge part of your final score. Asteroids are worth 2 points each, comets 3 points, gas clouds 4 points, and dwarf planets 4 points in Standard Mode. Plus, you earn 1 leader bonus point for each sector where you were first to submit the correct theory. Do not wait until you are 100 percent certain before submitting — you only get to submit during theory phases, and theories take multiple phases to reach peer review. If you are reasonably confident about a sector, submit the theory early to lock in the leader bonus. Just be aware that an incorrect theory costs you a 1-sector time penalty.

walkthrough.title: Tracking Opponents' Actions
walkthrough.text: Pay attention to what other players announce. When someone surveys for asteroids in sectors 7 through 12, note it down. You do not know the result, but you know they are gathering information about that range. If a player attempts to Locate Planet X and fails, that tells you they were wrong about either Planet X's sector or the adjacent sectors — useful information. When someone researches a topic, note which topics they have already covered so you can choose different ones and gather unique information.

walkthrough.title: The Truly Empty vs. Appears Empty Trap
walkthrough.text: This is the single most important distinction in the game. There are 2 truly empty sectors in Standard Mode and 5 in Expert Mode. But Planet X's sector also appears empty in surveys and targets. So when you survey for empty sectors and get a count of 2 in a range, that could mean 2 truly empty sectors, or 1 truly empty sector plus Planet X. Every time you see an empty result, ask yourself: could this be Planet X? This ambiguity is what makes locating Planet X challenging and is the core puzzle of the game.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Preparation
walkthrough.text: As you narrow down Planet X's location, start preparing for the Locate action. You need to identify three things: Planet X's sector, the object to its left, and the object to its right. This is where your saved target tokens become critical. Use one or both targets to confirm the adjacent sectors. Before attempting to locate, double-check all your deductions on your note sheet. The Locate action costs 5 time and a wrong guess pushes you far ahead on the time track with nothing to show for it. Also remember — once you correctly locate Planet X, other players behind you still get final scoring opportunities, so timing matters.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't use target tokens early — save them for the endgame
Don't survey narrow when a wider range costs the same time
Don't skip theory submissions — theories are a huge chunk of points
Don't rush Locate Planet X without confirming adjacent sectors

Planet X's sector also appears empty in surveys and targets

summary.title: Endgame Preparation
bullets: To Locate: identify Planet X's sector AND objects in both adjacent sectors
Use saved target tokens to confirm adjacent sectors before attempting
Locate costs 5 time — wrong guesses are very expensive
Other players get final scoring opportunities after you find Planet X

walkthrough.title: The Research-Heavy Opening
walkthrough.text: The strongest opening strategy is to lead with research. Research costs only 1 time, keeping you near the back of the time track and giving you more turns than opponents. On your first turn, research a topic. On your second turn, you cannot research again, so take a cheap survey. On your third turn, research a different topic. By the time you have researched three or four topics, you will have a web of logic rules that lets you start eliminating sectors en masse. Other players who opened with expensive surveys will be far ahead on the time track with fewer turns and fewer total clues.

walkthrough.title: Combining Clues for Cascading Deductions
walkthrough.text: Individual clues eliminate a few possibilities. Combined clues eliminate many. For example, if a research clue tells you all 4 asteroids are consecutive, and a survey tells you there are 0 asteroids in sectors 1-6, then all 4 asteroids must be in sectors 7-10, 8-11, 9-12, or 10-12-1-2 (wrapping around). If another survey narrows this further, you may pinpoint all 4 asteroid locations at once. This cascading effect is the key to efficient play — each new clue should trigger multiple deductions, not just one. Before taking a new action, always re-examine your existing clues with fresh eyes.

walkthrough.title: Survey Strategy: Targeted Ranges
walkthrough.text: The best surveys are designed to produce maximally useful results regardless of the number. Survey a range where every possible result (0, 1, 2, 3, etc.) gives you distinct deductive power. For asteroids, survey a range that overlaps with your suspected asteroid group — if you get 2, you know half the group is in that range and can narrow the other half. For comets, survey ranges that include multiple valid comet sectors. For empty sectors, survey ranges adjacent to where you suspect gas clouds, since gas clouds must neighbor truly empty sectors.

walkthrough.title: Using Object Rules as Constraints
walkthrough.text: Every object type has constraints that interact with each other. Asteroids must be adjacent to at least one other asteroid, so they form groups. Gas clouds must be adjacent to at least one truly empty sector, so they cluster near empty areas. Comets can only be in prime-numbered sectors. The dwarf planet cannot be adjacent to Planet X, and Planet X cannot be adjacent to a dwarf planet. Once you locate any object, you create constraints on neighboring sectors. If you know sector 5 has a gas cloud, one of its neighbors (4 or 6) must be truly empty. Working through these constraints systematically is faster than raw surveying.

walkthrough.title: Theory Timing for Maximum Points
walkthrough.text: Leader bonus points are awarded to the first player (or tied for first) to submit the correct theory in each sector. This means submitting early is critical for the bonus point — but submitting wrong costs a 1-sector penalty. The sweet spot is submitting theories as soon as you have strong deductive evidence, even if not absolute certainty. High-value targets like gas clouds (4 points) and dwarf planets (4 points in Standard) should be prioritized. Asteroids are only 2 points each, but there are 4 of them, so locking in all four asteroids early adds up to 8 theory points plus up to 4 leader bonuses.

walkthrough.title: Reading Opponents' Actions
walkthrough.text: Advanced players pay close attention to what opponents announce. If an opponent surveys for asteroids in sectors 7-12, they probably suspect asteroids are there — and their subsequent actions may confirm or deny this. If an opponent submits a theory in sector 9, they likely think they know what is there. If someone targets a sector, they are probably close to attempting to locate Planet X and want to confirm an adjacent sector. Use this publicly announced information to guide your own surveys and deductions. You may not know their results, but their choices reveal their thinking.

walkthrough.title: The Planet X Elimination Method
walkthrough.text: Rather than trying to find Planet X directly, eliminate where it cannot be. You know Planet X is not adjacent to a dwarf planet. You know the sector containing Planet X appears empty. Once you identify the dwarf planet's location, you can eliminate its two neighbors. Every truly empty sector you confirm is one less candidate for Planet X. Every sector confirmed as containing an object eliminates it. As you work through the logic, you will often narrow Planet X down to two or three possible sectors. Then use a targeted survey or your saved target tokens to distinguish between the remaining candidates.

walkthrough.title: Time Track Positioning in the Endgame
walkthrough.text: In the endgame, your position on the time track determines your final scoring opportunity if another player finds Planet X first. Being 4 or 5 sectors behind lets you submit up to 2 final theories, while being 1-3 sectors back limits you to 1 theory or one Locate attempt. If you think you are close to finding Planet X but another player might beat you, consider taking a cheap action to stay behind on the time track. This way, if they find it first, you still get a generous final opportunity. Conversely, if you are confident, rush to Locate before others can.

walkthrough.title: Expert Mode Adjustments
walkthrough.text: Expert Mode changes several key factors. The board has 18 sectors instead of 12, the visible sky is 9 sectors wide, and there are more objects: 4 dwarf planets instead of 1 (in a band of exactly 6 sectors with a dwarf planet at each end), and 5 truly empty sectors instead of 2. With more sectors and objects, deductions take longer and surveys cover proportionally less of the board. Research becomes even more valuable in Expert Mode. The 7-9 sector survey bracket at 2 time cost is extremely efficient. Theory phases allow submitting up to 2 theories instead of 1. Adjust your pacing accordingly — Expert games run longer.

walkthrough.title: The Final Locate: Getting It Right
walkthrough.text: When you are ready to attempt to Locate Planet X, run through this checklist. One: have you narrowed Planet X to a single sector through deduction, or are you guessing between two? If guessing, consider one more action to confirm. Two: do you know what is in both adjacent sectors? If not, use a target token. Three: check your note sheet for contradictions — sometimes a logic error sneaks in and a fresh review catches it. Four: consider the time track — will your attempt push you so far ahead that opponents get generous final scoring opportunities? When all four boxes are checked, go for it. Locating Planet X first is worth 10 points, the single biggest scoring event in the game.

entries.definition: One of 5 object types in the solar system. There are 4 asteroids total in both Standard and Expert Mode. Each asteroid must be adjacent to at least one other asteroid, meaning they form either two pairs or one group of four.

One of 5 object types. There are 2 comets total. In Standard Mode, each comet is located in one of sectors 2, 3, 5, 7, or 11. In Expert Mode, valid sectors are 2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13, or 17. When surveying for comets, the range must start and end with a valid comet sector.

One of 5 object types. In Standard Mode, there is 1 dwarf planet. In Expert Mode, there are 4 dwarf planets in a band of exactly 6 sectors, with a dwarf planet at each end. No dwarf planet is adjacent to Planet X.
One of 5 object types. There are 2 gas clouds total in both modes. Each gas cloud must be adjacent to at least one truly empty sector.

The hidden object players are trying to locate. There is 1 Planet X. It is not adjacent to a dwarf planet. Its sector appears empty in surveys and targets. To locate it, you must correctly identify its sector AND the objects in both adjacent sectors.
A sector that contains no object at all. There are 2 truly empty sectors in Standard Mode and 5 in Expert Mode. Truly empty sectors appear empty in surveys and targets, just like Planet X's sector.

A survey or target result meaning the sector either is truly empty or contains Planet X. This is the key distinction in the game — you cannot tell from a survey/target alone whether a sector is truly empty or holds Planet X.

The solar system is divided into individually numbered sectors arranged in numerical order around the board. Standard Mode has 12 sectors; Expert Mode has 18. Each sector contains exactly one object or is truly empty.

The main game board placed in the center of the table. It is double-sided — one side for Standard Mode, one for Expert Mode. The time track runs around its outer edge, and the sector numbers are on the inner ring.

A rotating overlay placed on top of the solar system board. It has a cutaway section that reveals the visible sky. It rotates clockwise to track the position of the active player's pawn. Conference and theory icons are marked on it.
A round piece placed in the center hole of the solar system board. The earth board sits on top of it and rotates around it.

The range of sectors visible through the cutaway in the earth board. Always exactly half the total sectors: 6 in Standard Mode, 9 in Expert Mode. Surveys and targets can only select sectors in the visible sky.
The track running around the outer edge of the solar system board. Player pawns advance clockwise on it as they take actions. The player whose pawn is farthest back is always the active player.

Each sector has its own 4-space peer review track. Theories are placed on the outer space and advance inward each theory phase. When a theory reaches the inner space, it undergoes peer review to determine if it is correct or incorrect.

Icons (X1, X2) on the earth board that trigger conference phases when the earth board rotates past them. Each conference reveals a public logic rule about Planet X's location to all players. Each conference triggers only once per game.
Icons on the earth board that trigger theory phases when the earth board rotates past them. During a theory phase, players may submit theories and existing theories advance on peer review tracks.

One of 4 actions. Select an object type and a range of consecutive sectors in the visible sky. The app reveals how many of that object are in that range. Announce type and range publicly; result is private. Time cost: 4 (1-3 sectors), 3 (4-6 sectors), or 2 (7-9 sectors, Expert only).

One of 4 actions. Select one sector in the visible sky. The app reveals which object is there or that it appears empty. Costs 1 target token (only 2 per game). Time cost: 4. Planet X appears empty when targeted.
One of 4 actions. Select a research topic from the app. The app reveals a logic rule for this game. Time cost: 1. Cannot be taken twice in a row. Each topic reveals only one piece of information.

One of 4 actions. Select the sector you believe contains Planet X and the objects in both adjacent sectors. The app reveals if you are correct. Time cost: 5. Correct guess triggers end of game. Announce success/failure but not the sector guessed.

Each action has a time cost that determines how many sectors your pawn advances on the time track. Locate Planet X: 5. Target: 4. Survey 1-3 sectors: 4. Survey 4-6 sectors: 3. Survey 7-9 sectors: 2 (Expert only). Research: 1.
The player whose pawn is farthest back on the time track. This player takes the next turn. If multiple pawns share a sector, the pawn in the rearmost position goes first.

Tokens in each player's color used to submit theories about which object is in a sector. Each player has 12 theory tokens. In Standard Mode, use only 1 dwarf planet token (return 3 to box). In Expert Mode, use all 4 dwarf planet tokens.

Each player has exactly 2 target tokens for the entire game. Spending a target token is required to take the Target a Sector action. Once both are spent, that action is unavailable for the rest of the game.
A screen in the player's color used to hide their note sheet and tokens from other players. In Expert Mode, an expert panel slides into slots in the screen for additional reference information.

A paper sheet where each player records clues, deductions, and theories. Each has a unique icon matching the player's view of the board. Has a grid for tracking objects by sector, spaces for research and conference clues, and a score chart.
A pawn in the player's chosen color that moves along the time track. Its position determines turn order and, at game end, the final scoring opportunities available to that player.

A required electronic app (available at www.PlanetXApp.com) that generates the random placement of objects, reveals survey results, research clues, target information, conference clues, peer review results, and Locate Planet X results. Each game has a unique game code.
A code generated by the companion app when starting a new game. All devices must enter the same game code to join the same game. Ensures all devices reference the same random object placement.

Part of the theory phase. In Standard Mode, each player may submit up to 1 theory per theory phase. In Expert Mode, up to 2. Theories are placed face down in player order (back to front on time track) on the outer space of the peer review track.

When a theory reaches the inner space of the peer review track, it is reviewed via the app. The app confirms whether the theory is correct or incorrect. Results are announced to all players. Correct theories confirm the object in that sector.

A theory confirmed by peer review. The object in that sector is now confirmed for all players. No more theories may be placed there. All other theories in that sector are flipped and resolved. Correct theories remain on the board for end-game scoring.

A theory disproven by peer review. The theory is removed from the game. The player who submitted it advances their pawn 1 sector on the time track as a penalty. All players note the disproven object for that sector.
End-game scoring. Score 1 point for each sector where you were the first player (or tied for first) to submit the correct theory. Submitting theories early is critical for earning these bonuses.
End-game scoring for each correct theory submitted. Asteroids: 2 points each. Comets: 3 points each. Gas clouds: 4 points each. Dwarf planets: 4 points in Standard Mode, 2 points each in Expert Mode.
End-game scoring. The first player to correctly locate Planet X scores 10 points. Other players who correctly locate later score 2 points per sector back from the first finder (2-10 points).

After Planet X is found, each player behind the finder gets one final action. 1-3 sectors back: submit 1 theory or attempt Locate. 4-5 sectors back: submit up to 2 theories or attempt Locate. Pawns do not advance during this phase.

A sector that is one position to the left or right of another sector on the circular board. For example, sectors 7 and 9 are adjacent to sector 8. Sector 12 is adjacent to sectors 11 and 1 (wrapping around).
An unbroken group of sectors without gaps. For example, sectors 9, 10, 11, 12 are consecutive. Used when describing asteroid groupings and survey ranges.
The one sector exactly halfway around the board (180 degrees away). In Standard Mode (12 sectors), sector 4 is directly opposite sector 10. Used in conference clues about Planet X's position.
Any sector up to N positions away in either direction. For example, sectors 'within 2 sectors' of sector 5 are sectors 3, 4, 6, and 7. Used in research topic clues.

entries.term: In a Band of N Sectors
entries.definition: An unbroken group of consecutive sectors of a given size. For example, sectors 7, 8, 9, 10 form a 'band of 4 sectors.' Used when describing how objects are clustered together, especially asteroids and Expert Mode dwarf planets.
The default game mode. 12 sectors, 6-sector visible sky, 1 dwarf planet, 2 truly empty sectors. Submit up to 1 theory per theory phase. Recommended for first-time players.

The advanced game mode. 18 sectors, 9-sector visible sky, 4 dwarf planets (in a band of 6 sectors with one at each end), 5 truly empty sectors. Submit up to 2 theories per theory phase. Surveys of 7-9 sectors cost 2 time.
A 1-player game variant. Complete only setup steps 1-11, skipping steps 12-16. Press 'Start Solo Game' from the app's initial screen for additional setup instructions and rules.
The app's recommended starting option for new players. Provides more starting information to give players an initial foothold for deductions.
Higher difficulty options in the app for returning players. Provides less starting information, making the deduction challenge harder.

A phase triggered when the earth board rotates past a conference icon (X1 or X2). The app reveals a logic rule about Planet X that is announced publicly to all players. Each conference triggers only once per game.
A phase triggered when the earth board rotates past a theory icon. Has two steps: (1) players submit theories, (2) theories that have reached the inner peer review space undergo peer review.

entries.term: Spring Equinox / Summer Solstice / Autumn Equinox / Winter Solstice
entries.definition: The four unique icons on the edges of the solar system board. Each player sits at one icon and uses the matching note sheet. These icons ensure each player views sector numbers correctly from their position.
An additional reference panel used only in Expert Mode. Slides into slots on the player screen. Provides extra reference information for the more complex 18-sector game.
